straight_beat.name: Raymond 'Ray' Tortorelli
straight_beat.column:
The Yankees lost yesterday, but the way they lost? That’s the kind of thing that keeps a guy like me up at night. Ten to eleven, Angels in the house, and yet here we are, still breathing, still in the race. The Bronx box isn’t just a metaphor—it’s a damn fortress, and even when the gates creak open, the walls hold.  

Let’s talk about the pitching staff for a sec. Taillon’s got that sink

optimist.name: Franca 'Frannie' Bellino
optimist.column:
The Yankees lost yesterday, but let’s not dwell on that. Let’s talk about the game, the players, and the fact that this team is still in the thick of the race. You know what I saw from Juan Soto yesterday? October in his eyes. By the time the leaves turn we’re having a parade, and I’m catering it.  

Now, I know the score: Angels 10, Yankees 11. But let’s not forget the heart of that game. Soto, that kid with the bat, didn’t just hit a home run—he hit a moment. A reminder that this team isn’t just about the old guard. They’ve got young talent, and talent doesn’t care about the scoreboard. It cares about the grind

pessimist.name: Sol Hirschberg
pessimist.column:
The Yankees lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to hurl your peanuts at the dugout. The Angels, of all teams, handed them a 10-11 defeat, and now they’re 9-7, three games behind the AL East. It’s not just the record that stings—it’s the fact that this team, with its payroll and star power, still hasn’t figured out how to win a playoff game. Cashman hands out extensions like Halloween candy, but when the dust settles, the Bombers are treading water. Feh.
